extra.message: User input from request.args.get('search') is concatenated directly into a SQL text() expression via f-string: text(f"text like '%{search_param}%'"). This allows SQL injection attacks through the 'search' query parameter.

User input from form.registration_code.data is concatenated directly into a raw SQL query via f-string: text(f"SELECT id, code FROM ... WHERE code = '{code}'"). This allows SQL injection attacks through the registration_code form field.

User-controlled cookie 'preferences' is base64-decoded and passed directly to pickle.loads(). An attacker can craft a malicious pickle payload in the cookie to achieve arbitrary code execution on the server.

User-supplied URL from form.url.data is passed directly to urllib.request.urlopen() without any validation or allowlist. An attacker can supply internal URLs (e.g., file://, http://localhost, http://169.254.169.254) to access internal resources or cloud metadata endpoints.

User input from request.path is interpolated into an f-string passed to render_template_string(). An attacker can craft a URL path containing Jinja2 template expressions (e.g., {{config}}) to achieve Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI), leading to arbitrary code execution.

Note text is rendered with the Jinja2 '| safe' filter: {{ note.text | safe }}. Since note text is user-supplied HTML from a CKEditor rich text field, an attacker can inject arbitrary JavaScript that will be executed in other users' browsers when viewing shared (non-private) notes.

The render_table macro is called with safe_columns=['text'], which disables HTML escaping for the 'text' column. Since note text is user-controlled, this enables stored XSS when search results are displayed.

The render_table macro is called with safe_columns=['text'], which disables HTML escaping for the 'text' column. Since note text is user-controlled, this enables stored XSS when personal notes are displayed.

The endpoint /accounts/<int:user_id>/notes allows any authenticated user to view another user's notes by changing the user_id parameter. There is no authorization check to verify the requesting user is authorized to view the specified user's notes.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The endpoint /notes/<int:note_id>/delete allows any authenticated user to delete any note by specifying its ID, without verifying that the current user owns the note or is an admin. The code checks note ownership in the template for displaying the delete button, but the server-side endpoint lacks this authorization check.
The POST /account endpoint (update_account) is missing the @login_required decorator. This means unauthenticated users can potentially send POST requests to update account information.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The update_account endpoint uses current_user.__dict__.update(filtered_values) with form data that includes the 'is_admin' field from AccountForm. An attacker can submit is_admin=true in the POST request to escalate their privileges to admin. The template only shows the is_admin checkbox to existing admins, but the server-side code does not restrict the field.

cwe: CWE-639: Authorization Bypass Through User-Controlled Key

extra.message: The Flask secret key is hardcoded as 'super secret key'. This allows an attacker to forge session cookies, CSRF tokens, and other signed data. The secret key should be loaded from an environment variable or secure configuration.

The database seed creates user accounts with extremely weak passwords: 'user' for user@evfa.com and 'admin' for admin@evfa.com. If this seed runs in production, these accounts are trivially compromised through password guessing.
A static registration code 'a36e990b-0024-4d55-b74a-f8d7528e1764' is hardcoded in the database seed. This code is visible in the source code and can be used by anyone to register an account.

All POST form endpoints (account update, note creation, note deletion, image upload, dark mode toggle, signup, login) lack CSRF protection. The application uses WTForms Form base class instead of FlaskForm, and there are no CSRF tokens in the HTML forms. This allows cross-site request forgery attacks.
SQLAlchemy engine is configured with echo=True, which logs all SQL statements including potentially sensitive data to the application output. This can leak sensitive information in production logs.

cwe: CWE-532: Insertion of Sensitive Information into Log File

extra.message: The POST /darkmode endpoint (toggle_darkmode) is missing the @login_required decorator. While lower severity on its own, combined with the insecure pickle deserialization in before_request, this could be part of an attack chain.

cwe: CWE-306: Missing Authentication for Critical Function

extra.message: The signup endpoint returns a distinct 'User already exists' message when a user tries to register with an existing email address. This allows an attacker to enumerate valid user email addresses.
